paper_title: DeepLoop: Depth Scaling for Looped Transformers

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Looped Transformers scale sequential computation by applying a compact stack of physical blocks for multiple rounds, increasing unrolled depth without increasing stored parameters. This reuse changes the residual-scaling problem: in an untied Transformer, each residual branch receives and applies its own parameter update, whereas in a looped Transformer one shared update aggregates gradients from repeated visits and is read back by those same visits in the next linearized forward pass. We formalize this tied-depth effect through a first-order perturbation bound controlled by a visit-alignment coefficient $κ_R$. The bound recovers the DeepNorm exponent when visits decorrelate, but in the conservative aligned regime it requires the exponent to increase from $1/4$ to $1/2$ as loop count grows at fixed physical depth. The resulting method, \textbf{DeepLoop}, keeps the Post-LN DeepNorm architecture and sets $α=(2N)^{1/2}$ and $β=(8N)^{-1/2}$ for unrolled depth $N$. On GPT-style looped language models at GPT-2 small and GPT-2 medium scale, DeepLoop is neutral when no physical block is revisited and improves validation loss and downstream accuracy once recurrent depth is activated. These results show that stable recurrent depth requires residual scaling rules that account for parameter visits, not only nominal layer count.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: DeepLoop: Depth Scaling for Looped Transformers Shuzhen Li1 Yifan Zhang1, Jiacheng Guo1 Quanquan Gu2 Mengdi Wang1, 1Princeton University 2University of California, Los Angeles Abstract Looped Transformers scale sequential computation by applying compact stack of physical blocks for multiple rounds, increasing unrolled depth without increasing stored parameters. This reuse changes the residual-scaling problem: in an untied Transformer, each residual branch receives and applies its own parameter update, whereas in looped Transformer one shared update aggregates gradients from repeated visits and is read back by those same visits in the next linearized forward pass. We formalize this tied-depth effect through first-order perturbation bound controlled by visit-alignment coefficient κR. The bound recovers the DeepNorm exponent when visits decorrelate, but in the conservative aligned regime it requires the exponent to increase from 1/4 to 1/2 as loop count grows at fixed physical depth. The resulting method, DeepLoop, keeps the Post-LN DeepNorm architecture and sets α = (2N )1/2 and β = (8N )1/2 for unrolled depth . On GPT-style looped language models at GPT-2 small and GPT-2 medium scale, DeepLoop is neutral when no physical block is revisited and improves validation loss and downstream accuracy once recurrent depth is activated. These results show that stable recurrent depth requires residual scaling rules that account for parameter visits, not only nominal layer count. Project Page: https://github.com/lszshu/DeepLoop

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Depth is one of the most reliable ways to improve Transformer expressivity, but in standard architectures, depth and parameter count grow together: adding layer also adds new set of attention and feed-forward weights (Vaswani et al., 2017; Kaplan et al., 2020; Hoffmann et al., 2022). Looped Transformers decouple these axes by applying stack of physical blocks for rounds, yielding unrolled depth = KR while storing only blocks (Figure 1). This mechanism connects classical depth-wise sharing in Universal Transformers, ALBERT, and Subformer (Dehghani et al., 2018; Lan et al., 2019; Reid et al., 2021) with recent recurrent-depth and test-time-compute models that spend additional sequential computation without proportional increase in parameters (Giannou et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023b; Gatmiry et al., 2024; Geiping et al., 2025; Saunshi et al., 2025). For the looped depth to become practical scaling axis, however, its residual parameterization must remain stable as the same physical blocks are revisited many times. The difficulty is that standard residual-scaling analyses are written for untied depth. Residual parameterization is decisive for deep Transformer optimization (Xiong et al., 2020; Nguyen and Corresponding authors: yifzhang@princeton.edu, mengdiw@princeton.edu. 1 (a) Physical blocks K=2 shared blocks, stored once Block 1 (ϕ1) Block 2 (ϕ2) attn ffn attn ffn loop rounds (b) Unrolled execution the same two blocks are revisited in every round x0 attn ffn attn ffn attn ffn attn ffn attn ffn attn ffn xN ϕ1 ϕ2 round r= ϕ1 ϕ2 round r=2 ϕ1 ϕ2 round r= R=3 rounds K=2 blocks unrolled depth =KR=6, =2N =12 sublayer visits (c) Residual sublayer: xi+1 = Norm(cid:0)α xi + fj(xi; ϕj)(cid:1) (d) Scaling rule xi skip α fj( ; ϕj) init gain β + Norm xi+1 DeepLoop (p = 1 2 ) α = (2N )1/2 β = (8N )1/ Figure 1 Overview of the DeepLoop framework. (a) Physical blocks: looped Transformer stores K=2 physical blocks (each with an attention and an FFN sublayer), Block 1 (ϕ1) and Block 2 (ϕ2). (b) Unrolled execution: The whole K-block stack is applied once per round for R=3 rounds, giving =KR=6 unrolled blocks and =2N =12 sublayer visits; the ϕj label shows which physical block is reused. (c) Residual sublayer: Each visit applies xi+1=Norm(α xi + fj(xi; ϕj)), with α scaling the skip connection and β the per-matrix initialization gain. (d) Scaling rule: DeepLoop sets α=(2N )1/2 and β=(8N )1/2. Salazar, 2019; Liu et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020; Bachlechner et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2024). DeepNorm, in particular, makes very deep Post-LN Transformers trainable by choosing skip scale α and residual-branch initialization gain β so that the first-order effect of parameter updates remains bounded across = 2N residual sublayer visits (Wang et al., 2024). This calculation assumes that each unrolled residual sublayer owns distinct parameter tensor and therefore contributes one independent update term. Weight sharing violates precisely this assumption. When physical sublayer is visited times, its optimizer update aggregates the visit-wise gradients from all rounds. The updated tensor is then reused by all of those visits in the next linearized forward computation. Tied depth therefore creates two coupled aggregation paths: the update is written by many visits and then read by many visits. The size of this effect depends on visit alignment. If visit-wise gradients and sensitivities are nearly orthogonal across rounds, looped model behaves like untied depth up to constants. If they are coherent, the shared update can acquire an additional factor of R. This paper makes the tied-depth effect explicit. We introduce visit-alignment coefficient κR and 2 show that the first-order stability condition for looped Transformer becomes κR (cid:19)2 (cid:18) β α = O(1), rather than the untied DeepNorm condition (β/α)2 = O(1). For the scaling family α = (cN )p and β = (dN )p, the usual DeepNorm threshold = 1/4 is recovered when visits decorrelate. In the conservative aligned regime, where κR = Θ(R) and is fixed while grows, the threshold becomes = 1/2. The resulting method is DeepLoop: keep the DeepNorm Post-LN architecture, but use the loop-aware scaling rule α = (2N )1/2, β = (8N )1/2. DeepLoop introduces no gates, learned residual coefficients, auxiliary losses, or architecture-specific tuning constants. It is deterministic residual parameterization for the regime where effective depth is increased by revisiting shared blocks. We evaluate DeepLoop in controlled GPT-style looped language modeling ablations at GPT-2 small and GPT-2 medium scales. DeepLoop is essentially neutral at = 1, where no physical block is revisited, and consistently improves final validation loss at larger loop counts. The advantage transfers to an eight-task language-model evaluation suite: downstream averages are close at = 1 and move in favor of DeepLoop as recurrent depth grows. These results support the central prediction of the analysis: residual scaling should depend on how depth is realized, not only on the nominal number of unrolled layers. Our contributions are: 1. We identify the tied-depth aggregation mechanism that is absent from untied residual-scaling analyses: shared update is accumulated across repeated visits and then read through those same visits; 2. We derive loop-aware first-order perturbation bound with an explicit visit-alignment coefficient κR, recovering DeepNorm in the decorrelated regime and yielding = 1/2 exponent threshold in the aligned fixed-physical-depth regime; 3. We propose DeepLoop, the scaling rule α = (2N )1/2 and β = (8N )1/2, as one-line conservative parameterization for Post-LN looped Transformers; 4. We provide controlled pre-training and downstream ablations at GPT-2 small and GPT-2 medium scale, showing that the correction becomes useful precisely when the loop count is greater than one.

sections.title: 2.1 Looped Transformers and effective depth
sections.content: A standard depth-N Transformer applies distinct blocks once. looped Transformer instead chooses physical blocks and applies them for rounds, xr,k+1 = Bk(xr,k; ϕk), = 1, . . . , K, = 1, . . . , R, xr+1,1 = xr,K+1, (2.1) so the effective depth is = KR while the block parameters ϕ1, . . . , ϕK are stored only once. The Universal Transformer (Dehghani et al., 2018) corresponds to the fully recurrent extreme in which 3 the same transition is reused across depth; ALBERT (Lan et al., 2019) similarly ties Transformer parameters across layers. The regime studied here keeps fixed or small and increases R, thereby increasing test-time compute and unrolled depth without increasing the number of physical blocks.

sections.title: 2.2 Residual normalization
sections.content: Let gℓ denote one residual sublayer, either attention or an MLP. The two common normalization placements are Pre-LN: xℓ+1 = xℓ + gℓ(Norm(xℓ)), Post-LN: xℓ+1 = Norm (xℓ + gℓ(xℓ)) . Pre-LN improves optimization stability in deep Transformers, while Post-LN can preserve more expressive residual stream (Xiong et al., 2020; Nguyen and Salazar, 2019). DeepNorm (Wang et al., 2024) modifies the Post-LN residual path by scaling the skip connection before normalization: xℓ+1 = Norm (α xℓ + gℓ(xℓ; θℓ)) . (2.2) For an encoder-only or decoder-only Transformer with blocks and = 2N residual sublayer applications, DeepNorm sets α = (2N )1/4, β = (8N )1/4, (2.3) Here β is an initialization gain, not an additional runtime multiplier on the residual stream. If DN denotes the residual-branch matrices scaled by DeepNet in sublayer ℓ, such as the value and output projections in attention and the feed-forward matrices, then DeepNorm initializes ℓ (0) ℓ,q = βDN (cid:102)W (0) ℓ,q , DN ℓ , βDN = (8N )1/4, (2.4) with the usual unscaled initializer used for (cid:102)W (0) is therefore ℓ,q . The quantity that enters the perturbation argument βDN αDN = (8N )1/4 (2N )1/ = 1 2 . (2.5) The useful way to summarize the DeepNorm calculation is not bound on α2β, but the first-order update condition (cid:19)2 (cid:18) β α = Θ(1). (2.6) Indeed, substituting Eq. (2.5) gives 2N (β/α)2 = 1/2.

sections.title: 2.3 Why weight sharing changes the depth scaling
sections.content: In non-shared depth-N Transformer, each sublayer parameter receives one gradient contribution per input. In looped Transformer, the same physical parameter is visited once per round, so its gradient is sum over visits. If the visit-wise contributions decorrelate, the loop behaves like untied depth up to constants. If the visits are aligned, the tied update can be times larger, and that same update is then read by all visits in the unrolled computation. DeepLoop is the corresponding conservative correction to Eq. (2.6): it chooses smaller update-to-residual ratio β/α so that the first-order effect of the tied parameter update remains bounded after all visits are unrolled.

sections.title: 3 DeepLoop Transformer
sections.content: A looped Transformer reuses the same physical blocks for rounds. Its unrolled depth is = KR, and the number of residual-sublayer visits is = 2N . DeepLoop keeps the DeepNorm architecture but uses the exponent = 1/2 rather than = 1/4 in the fixed-physical-depth, increasing-loop-count regime. The derivation separates two facts: RMSNorm exposes the residual branch through factor 1/α, and parameter tying changes the first-order update sum from terms to κR terms, where κR measures alignment across loop visits.

sections.title: 3.1 Setup: looped post-normalized block
sections.content: Each physical block contains two residual sublayers: attention and MLP. Let = (k, s) index physical sublayer, with {1, . . . , K} and {attn, ffn}, and let = (r, j) denote its r-th unrolled visit. We write = 2K for the number of physical residual sublayers and = JR = 2KR = 2N for the number of unrolled visits. DeepLoop uses xi+1 = Norm (α xi + fj(xi; ϕj)) , = 1, . . . , M, where the same physical parameter ϕj is reused for all = 1, . . . , R. In our implementation, Norm is RMSNorm. The gain β is per-matrix initialization gain applied to the residual-branch matrices specified by DeepNorm. It should not be read as the standard deviation of the whole sublayer output, since branch may contain multiple scaled matrices. Concretely, for each physical sublayer j, let Sj be the set of matrices that receive the DeepNorm gain. DeepLoop initializes (0) j,q = βDL (cid:102)W (0) j,q , Sj, βDL = (8N )1/2. (3.1) The forward recurrence then uses these initialized parameters through fj(xi; ϕj) at every visit; β is not re-applied as separate multiplicative factor on each visit. With αDL = (2N )1/2, the DeepLoop update-to-residual ratio is βDL αDL = (8N )1/2 (2N )1/2 = 1 4N . (3.2) Assumption 3.1 (DeepNorm-scale local sensitivity). For each unrolled visit = (r, j), the norm of the linearized output map from residual-branch parameter perturbation at that visit and the norm of the corresponding visit-wise effective update are both O(β/α). Assumption 3.1 is the local scaling condition used in the DeepNet perturbation argument. Constants depending on width, normalization gain, attention heads, learning rate, or optimizer preconditioning are absorbed into the O() notation. The looped analysis below changes only how the visit-wise terms are aggregated when parameters are tied.

sections.title: 3.2 Forward stability
sections.content: RMSNorm restores unit RMS at every sublayer, so the forward signal scale is not the binding constraint; the relevant constraint is the first-order sensitivity of the final output to residual-branch parameter update. direct expansion of RMSNorm(αx + z) around RMS(z)/α 0 shows that the residual branch enters the normalized direction through factor of 1/α, which is why the relevant DeepNorm ratio is β/α rather than β alone. The formal statement and proof are Lemma A.1 in Appendix A.

sections.title: 3.3 Depth-untied DeepNorm bound
sections.content: The DeepNet perturbation argument bounds the first-order output change = (x; θ+δθ)F (x; θ) by summing visit-wise sensitivities. Here depth-untied means that residual-sublayer parameters are not shared across unrolled depth. This is orthogonal to the standard tying of input and output token embeddings: tied embeddings, when used, are outside the = 2N residual-sublayer count and are not among the residual-branch matrices scaled by β. Under Assumption 3.1, each visit contributes an output sensitivity O(β/α) and an effective update O(β/α), so summing = 2N depth-untied residual-sublayer visits gives and sufficient first-order stability condition is (cid:18) β α (cid:19)2 = O(1). C (cid:19)2 (cid:18) β α , (3.3) (3.4) The DeepNorm choice α = (2N )1/4 and β = (8N )1/4 satisfies (β/α)2 = 1/2. The formal proposition statement and proof are Proposition A.2 in Appendix A.

sections.title: 3.4 Loop correction: aligned visits require p = 1/2
sections.content: Depth-wise residual-sublayer weight sharing changes Eq. (3.3). For physical sublayer j, let Gr,j be the visit-wise effective update that would be applied if visit (r, j) had its own depth-untied residualbranch parameter, and let Ur,j be the corresponding linearized output-sensitivity operator. With tied residual-sublayer parameters, the update to ϕj is proportional to (cid:80)R r=1 Gr,j, and the output perturbation reads this same update through all visits. The first-order tied perturbation therefore has the schematic double-sum form Ftied = η (cid:88) (cid:32) (cid:88) (cid:33) (cid:32) (cid:88) Ur,j j=1 r=1 t=1 (cid:33) Gt,j + O(η2), (3.5) where tensor contractions and optimizer-dependent constants are absorbed into Ur,j and Gr,j. The formal proposition and the aligned-case corollary are Proposition A.3 and Corollary A.4 in Appendix A. Now consider the family α = (cN )p, β = (dN )p with constants c, > 0. Its update-to-residual ratio is β α = (cd)pN 2p. (3.9) Proposition 3.2 (Exponent threshold). Assume is fixed, = KR, = 2N , and κR = Θ(Rγ) for some γ [0, 1]. For the scaling family α = (cN )p, β = (dN )p, the tied-depth condition holds uniformly as if and only if κR(β/α)2 = O(1) 1 + γ 4 . Proposition 3.2 shows where the new exponent comes from. When visits decorrelate, γ = 0, and the DeepNorm threshold = 1/4 is recovered. In the conservative aligned case, γ = 1, so the threshold is = 1/2. Equivalently,

Proofs of Proposition A.3 and Proposition 3.2 are in Appendix A. Appendix sweeps over single grid at R=3 and finds an empirical training-stability boundary that lines up with this p=1/2 prediction.

sections.title: 3.5 Summary and comparison to DeepNorm
sections.content: Residual scale α Init gain β Per-matrix use of β Update-to-residual β/α Untied bound (β/α)2 Aligned tied-loop bound R(β/α)2 at fixed

sections.title: DeepLoop
sections.content: (2N )1/4 (8N )1/4 (2N )1/2 (8N )1/2 (0) β (cid:102)W (0) (0) β (cid:102)W (0) 1/(2 ) Θ(1) Θ(R) 1/(4N ) Θ(N 1) Θ(1/K) DeepLoop is therefore one-line conservative correction for tied depth: increase the exponent from = 1/4 to = 1/2. It introduces no gates, learnable residual scalars, or additional tuning constants. The correction is not claim that every looped model empirically attains worst-case alignment. Rather, it is the minimal exponent for uniform boundedness under aligned visit-wise contributions at fixed physical depth.

sections.title: 4 Application to Hierarchical Recurrent Reasoners
sections.content: The DeepLoop derivation in Section 3 treats single looped block visited for rounds. Recent recurrent-reasoning architectures combine looping with two further structural choices: hierarchy of recurrent modules executed at different rates, and training-time gradient truncation across the outer recurrence (Wang et al., 2025; Geiping et al., 2025). The Hierarchical Reasoning Model (Wang et al., 2025) instantiates both: high-level module and low-level module are unrolled jointly, and gradients are computed through only the last outer cycle. This section shows that the framework of Section 3 extends to this regime by replacing the unrolled visit count with gradient-visible visit count Mg and tracking per-module visit counts separately. We do not introduce new optimization machinery or new variant of DeepLoop; we describe how the existing analysis specializes when training-time depth and forward depth differ.

sections.title: 4.1 Setup: two-module hierarchical loops
sections.content: Let contain KH physical blocks (each with two residual sublayers, attention and MLP) and let contain KL physical blocks of the same shape. The hierarchical recurrence runs outer cycles; within each outer cycle, iterates CL times before single update of H: cycle : zL L(CL)(zL, zH ), zH H(zH , zL), = 1, . . . , C. Counting attention and MLP residual sublayers, the total unrolled visit count is = 2 (KH + CLKL), (4.1) which generalizes the single-module count = 2KR used in Section 3. The set of distinct physical residual sublayers has size = JH + JL with JH = 2KH and JL = 2KL. Each visit = (c, ℓ, j) has an outer-cycle index c, an inner L-iterate index ℓ (only meaningful for L), and physical-sublayer index j.

sections.title: 4.2 Loop-aware bound under one-step gradient approximation
sections.content: Hierarchical recurrent reasoners are typically trained with one-step gradient approximation: the forward pass uses all outer cycles, but the backward pass is computed against the last cycle only, with zH and zL from earlier cycles detached from the autograd graph. This truncation does not alter the numerical forward values, so Lemma A.1 applies unchanged locally. The perturbation bound below is for the truncated training graph: the states entering the last outer cycle are treated as fixed inputs, and both the sensitivity terms and the update terms from earlier cycles are absent. perturbation bound for the full untruncated forward map after an optimizer step would require an additional forward-visible read factor. 8 Define the gradient-visible visit count Mg := 2 (KH + CLKL), so = Mg. The per-physical-sublayer round counts on the gradient-visible cycle are R(H) = 1, R(L) = CL. (4.2) (4.3) Repeating the argument leading to Eq. (3.7) but restricting the visit-index sums to the gradient-visible cycle gives the two-module loop-aware bound C(cid:104) JH R(H) κ(H) + JL R(L) κ(L) (cid:105) (cid:18) β α (cid:19)2 , (4.4) , κ(L) where κ(H) are the visit-alignment coefficients of Eq. (3.6) restricted to each modules gradientvisible visits. Two structural facts simplify Eq. (4.4). First, κ(H) = 1, each physical H-sublayer contributes single-term inner sum, and the alignment coefficient collapses to constant. The truncation thus places in the untied-DeepNorm regime even though is forward-visited times. Second, κ(L) [0, CL] by the triangle inequality applied to the inner L-iterate sum. Substituting these facts, upper-bounding κ(H) by one, and applying the per-module counts in Eq. (4.3) yields the sufficient stability condition 1 trivially: with R(H) Mg κg (cid:19)2 (cid:18) β α = O(1), κg := JH + JL CL κ(L) Mg , (4.5) which has the same structural form as Eq. (3.8) with replaced by Mg and κR replaced by the gradient-visible aggregate κg [ JH /Mg, 1 + JLCL(CL 1)/Mg ]. The decomposition (4.4) also licenses per-module scaling family. Each summand depends only on the (α, β) used at the corresponding module, so hierarchical reasoner may be parameterized with (αH, βH) and (αL, βL) chosen independently. The admissible exponent at each module is determined by the corresponding summand of Eq. (4.4), not by the global visit count.

sections.title: 4.3 Predicted exponent regime
sections.content: Let Ng := Mg/2 denote the gradient-visible block-equivalent depth. Combining Eq. (4.5) with the same two-sublayer scaling family used above, α = (cNg)p, β = (dNg)p, gives, up to constant factors, Mg κg (cd)2p 4p = Θ(cid:0)(cd)2p κg 14p (cid:1) , (4.6) and the asymptotic threshold for boundedness depends on how κg and Mg co-scale. We record the two limit cases that mirror Proposition 3.2. Decorrelated inner cycles (κ(L) = O(1)). Then κg = O(1) and Eq. (4.6) is bounded as Mg if and only if 1/4. Both modules sit in the untied-DeepNorm regime: the module by truncation, and the module by the inner-cycle decorrelation. Aligned inner cycles (κ(L) JL/Mg). Two subcases follow from how Mg is grown. (i) If CL is fixed and KH , KL , then κg = Θ(1) and the = Θ(CL)). Then the contribution to κg is Θ(C2 9 threshold is again 1/4. (ii) If KH , KL are fixed and CL , then Mg = Θ(CL) and κg = Θ(CL), so Eq. (4.6) is bounded if and only if 1/2, which recovers the fixed-physical-depth tied-loop threshold of Proposition 3.2 along the inner-cycle axis. For hierarchical recurrent reasoner as actually built, these two limits are not equally likely possibilities but binding constraint and slack one. The once-per-step module is truncated by the one-step gradient, contributes single update term, and therefore requires only 1/4. The module is the one that realizes the models effective depth: it revisits its shared blocks at fixed physical depth, the CL axis of sub-case (ii), and weight tying is adopted precisely so that those revisits implement the same operation, which aligns their visit-wise gradients and sensitivities (κ(L) = Θ(CL)). The module therefore requires 1/2. single shared residual exponent must satisfy the more demanding module, so the framework predicts = 1/ (4.7) for hierarchical recurrent reasoner, the same loop-aware exponent as the single-module backbone of Section 3. This is statement about the architecture as trained, not conservative envelope: the recurrent depth that makes the model work is grown along the aligned inner-cycle axis, exactly the regime whose threshold is 1/2, and = 1/2 simultaneously satisfies the once-per-step module (for which 1/2 1/4). Section 5 confirms the prediction empirically on ARC-AGI. Forward signal at the loop entry. practical detail of hierarchical reasoners is the addition of learned task or puzzle embedding etask to the token embedding etok before the loop. The choice of whether to apply RMSNorm to etok + etask at the loop entry affects only the forward signal scale RMS(x0) in Lemma A.1; it does not enter Eq. (4.4) or the threshold derivation (4.6). Inputside normalization and the residual scaling rule are therefore separable design choices within this framework, even though both can independently influence the relative magnitude of task-conditioning information across repeated visits.

sections.title: 4.4 Summary
sections.content: The DeepLoop perturbation argument extends to hierarchical recurrent reasoners by two substitutions. When training uses one-step gradient approximation, the visit count entering the bound is the gradient-visible Mg from Eq. (4.2) rather than the forward from Eq. (4.1). When the architecture splits the recurrence across modules with different per-step visit counts, the bound decomposes into per-module summands and admits asymmetric per-module (α, β) assignments. Because hierarchical recurrent reasoner grows its effective depth by aligned revisits of the shared inner module at fixed physical depth, the binding threshold from Eq. (4.6) is = 1/2, the same loop-aware exponent as the single-module backbone. Section 5 confirms this on ARC-AGI: the DeepLoop exponent = 1/2 improves voted accuracy over the vanilla HRM baseline across the full evaluation ladder.

sections.title: 5.1 Validation loss
sections.content: We compare DeepLoop against baseline that already incorporates the same looped residual-block sharing, tied input-output embeddings, and an input-embedding RMSNorm. The baseline (base) uses this same looped backbone and embedding tying, but keeps the residual and initialization scales 10 at α = 1 and β = 1. The DeepLoop variant additionally enables the scaling rule on every block. Both configurations share an identical backbone, optimizer, data pipeline, and random seed; the only knob that moves between rows of Table 1 is the loop count {1, 3, 5, 7}, and the only knob that moves between methods is the activation of DeepLoop. The GPT-2 small runs use GPT-MHA-RoPE model trained on FineWeb-Edu for 50B tokens (100K optimizer steps, context length 1024, global batch size 480) on 4H200 141GB GPUs. The GPT-2 medium runs use the same backbone with hidden size enlarged from 768 to 1024 and the number of layers doubled from 12 to 24, trained on the same data and schedule on 8H200 141GB GPUs. We report the validation cross-entropy at the final checkpoint for both scales in Table 1. Table 1 Final validation loss at step 100,000 on FineWeb-Edu 50BT for the GPT-2 small and GPT-2 medium backbones, varying loop count {1, 3, 5, 7}. Bold marks the best cell per column per scale; ties within 0.002 nats are bolded on both rows. DeepLoop is effectively tied with the baseline at R=1 and improves over it in these single-seed runs at R=3, R=5, and R=7 at both scales. Method = 1 = 3 = 5 = 7 GPT-2 small backbone baseline (tying + input-embed RMSNorm) DeepLoop (DeepLoop base) GPT-2 medium backbone baseline (tying + input-embed RMSNorm) DeepLoop (DeepLoop base) 2.7700 2.8627 2.8631 2.7514 +0.0004 0.0160 0.0231 0.0186 2.7910 2.7679 2.8077 2.7917 2.5558 2.6253 2.6264 2.5280 +0.0011 0.0153 0.0196 0.0278 2.5640 2.5444 2.5779 2. Figure 2 visualizes the same numbers as Table 1. At both scales the two curves coincide within noise at R=1 (+0.0004 and +0.0011 nats) and separate as the loop count grows: at the small scale DeepLoop pulls ahead by 0.016 nats at R=3, the gap peaks at 0.023 nats at R=5, and remains open at 0.019 nats at R=7; at the medium scale the gap widens monotonically through R=7, reaching 0.028 nats. Both methods continue to improve monotonically with at both scales, and DeepLoop strictly beats the baseline at every 3. Multi-seed runs would be needed to quantify run-to-run variance.

sections.title: 5.2 Downstream evaluation on the lm-evaluation-harness suite
sections.content: To test whether the validation-loss advantage transfers to downstream task accuracy, we evaluate the eight medium-scale checkpoints from 5.1 on the eight-task zeroand one-shot suite used by Zhang et al. (2026) in the Deep Delta Learning paper: arc_challenge, arc_easy, hellaswag, openbookqa, piqa, sciq, social_iqa, and winogrande. Evaluation uses the lm-evaluation-harness (Gao et al., 2024) with its default acc metric (not acc_norm), the GPT-2 tokenizer, context length of 1024 (matching training), and bf16 inference. The reported Avg column is the unweighted arithmetic mean of the eight per-task accuracies, following the protocol of Zhang et al. (2026). Table 2 reports the per-task accuracy and Avg for every (method, R, shot) cell at the medium scale. Figure 3 breaks the 1-shot column out task-by-task. The corresponding GPT-2 small downstream results, which exhibit the same qualitative pattern, are deferred to Appendix B. Two takeaways. First, the qualitative picture from the small-scale ablation (Appendix Table 4) carries over to the larger backbone: the two methods are tied at R=1 ( = 0.01 on the 0-shot 11 Figure 2 Final validation loss (step 100,000) against loop count {1, 3, 5, 7} on FineWeb-Edu 50BT for the GPT-2 small (left) and GPT-2 medium (right) backbones. DeepLoop matches the baseline at R=1 and improves over it at every 3 at both scales in these single-seed runs. Data are the same as Table 1. Avg, 0.16 on 1-shot), DeepLoop opens the gap at R=3 (+0.43 / +0.32), and the best Avg cell in the table is DeepLoop R=7 in both shot settings, headlined by 55.20 % on 1-shot. On the 0-shot Avg the gap widens through R=7 (+0.43, +1.06, +0.93); on the 1-shot Avg the ranking is messier at R=5 (where the base reaches 54.42 %, 0.23 points above DeepLoop) but DeepLoop recovers the lead by +0.58 points at R=7. Second, at R=7 DeepLoop beats the same-R baseline on seven of the eight individual tasks under both shot settings (only PIQA goes the other way), with WinoGrande in particular jumping by +1.74 points 0-shot (59.04 vs. 57.30). The same caveat about single-seed run-to-run variance from 5.1 applies, we see no qualitative disagreement with the small-scale ablation, only stronger depth signal.

sections.title: 5.3 Reasoning evaluation on ARC-AGI
sections.content: To test the prediction of Eq. (4.7) on hierarchical recurrent reasoner, we apply the DeepLoop scaling rule with = 1/2 to the Hierarchical Reasoning Model (HRM) (Wang et al., 2025) and evaluate on ARC-AGI-1 (Chollet, 2019). The only change from the published HRM is the residual parameterization: every reasoning block uses α = (2Ng)1/2 and β = (8Ng)1/2 with Mg = 24 gradient-visible residual-sublayer visits and Ng = Mg/2 = 12 block-equivalent depth (Eq. 4.2); the backbone, the adaptive-computation halting, the optimizer (AdamATan2), the data pipeline, and the 100K-epoch schedule are held fixed. Baseline and DeepLoop runs train on an identical, hash-verified arc-aug-1000 build and are scored by the same count-first voting protocol on = 400 evaluation puzzles. As harness check, the published HRM ARC-2 checkpoint scores 5.00% (K=2) on this pipeline, matching the released value. Table 3 reports voted accuracy at voting budgets {1, 2, 10, 100, 1000}. DeepLoop improves the paper-protocol two-vote accuracy from 36.50% to 39.75% (+3.25 pp) and improves every column of the voting ladder. four-seed control places the per-seed K=2 standard deviation at 0.5 pp, so the +3.25 pp gain is roughly 6σ effect rather than seed draw. The prediction that tied 12 Table 2 Downstream accuracy (%) for the GPT-2 medium backbone on FineWeb-Edu 50BT, mirroring Table 4 but extended to R=7. Metric is acc; Avg is the unweighted arithmetic mean across the eight tasks. Bold marks the best cell per column per shot setting. Table 3 Voted accuracy (%) on ARC-AGI-1 for the vanilla Hierarchical Reasoning Model and the same model with the DeepLoop residual scaling (p = 1/2), at voting budgets K. Both runs use 100K epochs, AdamATan2, and an identical hash-verified arc-aug-1000 build; = 400 evaluation puzzles. Bold marks the better cell per column; the K=2 column is the paper-protocol headline metric. Method K=1 K=2 K=10 K=100 K= Vanilla HRM DeepLoop (p=1/2) 41.50 36.50 31.50 35.50 44.25 39.75 +4.00 +3.25 +2.75 47.50 49.75 +2.25 50.75 51.50 +0.

sections.title: 6 Related Work
sections.content: Looped and universal Transformers. Universal Transformers (Dehghani et al., 2018) and ALBERT (Lan et al., 2019) introduced depth-wise parameter sharing as way to increase effective computation without increasing parameter count. Later work studied partial sharing (Reid et al., 2021), looped Transformers for in-context learning and algorithmic computation (Giannou et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023b; Gatmiry et al., 2024), and recurrent-depth language models that trade inference compute for 13 Figure 3 Per-task 1-shot accuracy against loop count for base versus DeepLoop on all eight tasks at the GPT-2 medium scale. At R=7 DeepLoop wins on seven of the eight tasks under both shot settings; see Table 2 for exact values. quality (Geiping et al., 2025). DeepLoop is orthogonal to these architectures: it only changes the residual and initialization scales used when physical block is revisited. Adaptive computation and iterative reasoning. Looped depth can also be viewed as differentiable test-time-compute mechanism. Adaptive Computation Time (Graves, 2016), PonderNet (Banino et al., 2021), and recurrent networks for algorithmic extrapolation (Schwarzschild et al., 2021; Bansal et al., 2022) all exploit repeated application of shared computation. Recent languagemodeling work similarly adds latent or recurrent computation (Goyal et al., 2023; Hao et al., 2024; Saunshi et al., 2025). Our analysis targets the stability of this repeated shared computation. Residual scaling and parameterization. Transformer stability has been improved through normalization placement (Xiong et al., 2020; Nguyen and Salazar, 2019), initialization and residual scaling (Zhang et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2020; De and Smith, 2020; Bachlechner et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020), and DeepNorm/DeepNet (Wang et al., 2024), which derives α = (2N )1/4 and β = (8N )1/4 for untied Post-LN Transformers. Width-and-depth parameterizations such as µP and Depth-µP prescribe complementary scalings for feature learning and hyperparameter transfer (Yang and Hu, 2021; Yang et al., 2021, 2023a; Bordelon et al., 2023). These analyses do not explicitly account for the extra correlation introduced when the same residual-branch parameters are reused across loop visits. DeepLoop is loop-specific correction to the DeepNorm exponent.

sections.title: 7 Conclusion
sections.content: Looped Transformers turn depth into controllable compute resource, but repeated parameter reuse changes the residual-scaling problem. When residual branch is revisited, its shared update is 14 accumulated across visits and then read by those same visits in the next linearized forward pass. We captured this tied-depth effect with visit-alignment coefficient κR, recovering the DeepNorm exponent for decorrelated visits and identifying = 1/2 as the conservative exponent for aligned visits at fixed physical depth. DeepLoop implements this correction with one-line Post-LN scaling rule, α = (2N )1/2 and β = (8N )1/2. Empirically, it is neutral when no physical block is revisited and improves looped GPT-style language models as recurrent depth increases, with gains in validation loss and downstream accuracy. p-sweep at R=3 further places the stability boundary near = 1/2. Future work should measure κR or cross-round gradient alignment directly, test whether the same boundary holds at larger scale or under alternative parameterizations, and explore whether training can encourage decorrelated visits to safely use less conservative exponents.

sections.title: A Proofs
sections.content: Lemma A.1 (RMSNorm exposes the residual branch through 1/α). Let RMS(x)2 = d1x2, assume RMS(x) = 1, and define R(y) = y/RMS(y). If RMS(z)/α < 1, then R(αx + z) = + x, zdx α + (cid:18) RMS(z)2 α2 (cid:19) , x, zd := d1xz. (A.1) Proof of Lemma A.1. Write = x, zd and δ2 = RMS(z)2. Since δ and δ/α < 1, the quantity below is bounded away from zero: RMS(αx + z) = α (cid:0)1 + 2m/α + δ2/α2(cid:1)1/2 . first-order Taylor expansion of the inverse square root around one gives (cid:0)1 + 2m/α + δ2/α2(cid:1)1/2 = 1 m/α + O(δ2/α2), where the remainder is uniform for δ/α c. Multiplying by (αx + z)/α yields αx + RMS(αx + z) = + mx α + O(δ2/α2), which is Eq. (A.1). Proposition A.2 (Depth-untied first-order stability). For depth-N post-normalized Transformer whose residual-sublayer parameters are not shared across unrolled depth, with = 2N residualsublayer visits, Assumption 3.1 implies the first-order bound Eq. (3.3), and consequently (β/α)2 = O(1) (Eq. (3.4)) is sufficient first-order stability condition. This statement does not depend on whether the input and output token embeddings are tied. Proof of Proposition A.2. In the depth-untied residual stack, every unrolled residual-sublayer visit has its own residual-branch parameter tensor. The first-order change is therefore sum of independent visit-wise perturbation terms. By Assumption 3.1, each term is bounded by an output sensitivity O(β/α) times an effective update O(β/α). Summing over visits gives CM (β/α)2, for depth-independent constant C, which implies the stated sufficient condition. Proposition A.3 (Tied loop perturbation). Under Assumption 3.1, looped Transformer with = 2K physical residual sublayers and visits satisfies the first-order bound Eq. (3.7), so κR(β/α)2 = O(1) (Eq. (3.8)) is sufficient tied-depth stability condition. Corollary A.4 (Worst-case aligned loop bound). If κR = Θ(R), then Eq. (3.8) reduces to R(β/α)2 = O(1). Proof of Proposition A.3. Using Eq. (3.5) and submultiplicativity, Ftied η (cid:88) j=1 (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:88) r=1 Ur,j (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) 19 (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:88) t=1 Gt,j (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13) + O(η2). By the definition of κR, each physical sublayer contributes at most RκRCU CG(β/α)2. Summing over physical sublayers gives JRκRCU CG(β/α)2 = κRCU CG(β/α)2. Absorbing η, CU , and CG into the constant proves Eq. (3.7). Proof of Proposition 3.2. Substituting Eq. (3.9) into the tied-depth condition gives κR (cid:19)2 (cid:18) β α = 2N Θ(Rγ) (cd)2pN 4p. At fixed physical depth K, = N/K, so the -dependence is Θ(N 1+γ4p). This quantity remains uniformly bounded as if and only if 1 + γ 4p 0, equivalently (1 + γ)/4. 20 Small-scale downstream evaluation We report the GPT-2 small version of the downstream evaluation from 5.2. The architecture is identical to the medium backbone except for hidden size 768 (vs. 1024) and 12 layers (vs. 24); training uses the same FineWeb-Edu 50BT data and schedule but on 4H200 141 GB GPUs. The same eight-task lm-evaluation-harness protocol applies. Table 4 reports per-task accuracy and Avg; Figures 4 and 5 visualize the 1-shot Avg and the per-task 1-shot accuracy, respectively. Table 4 Downstream accuracy (%) for the GPT-2 small backbone. Metric is acc; Avg is the unweighted arithmetic mean across the eight tasks. Bold marks the best cell per column per shot setting. Method / ARC-C ARC-E HellaSwag OBQA PIQA SciQ SIQA WG Avg 0-shot base R=1 base R=3 base R=5 DeepLoop R=1 DeepLoop R=3 DeepLoop R=5 1-shot base R=1 base R=3 base R=5 DeepLoop R=1 DeepLoop R=3 DeepLoop R= 23.12 23.89 26.62 24.49 26.96 25.09 26.11 24.74 26.88 25.26 26.88 28.24 57.11 60.40 61.49 58.38 60.31 61.36 57.95 61.28 62.08 57.15 61.41 62.67 31.95 33.70 34.14 32.31 33.98 34.86 31.95 33.42 33.92 31.90 33.91 34. 20.60 20.20 20.80 20.40 22.00 21.00 19.80 20.80 23.20 21.00 21.00 22.60 65.83 66.76 66.49 65.83 67.79 68.50 65.78 67.68 67.52 67.14 67.46 67.41 81.90 83.20 83.80 80.90 83.80 82.20 83.50 86.80 87.10 82.80 87.10 87. 37.87 37.21 38.54 38.23 39.87 39.46 38.38 37.87 40.02 38.89 39.76 40.02 51.62 52.09 53.12 51.30 53.20 53.75 52.57 52.41 52.57 51.30 53.83 54.70 46.25 47.18 48.12 46.48 48.49 48.28 47.00 48.13 49.16 46.93 48.92 49. The two methods are within 0.25 points of each other at R=1 on both shot settings, and DeepLoop has higher Avg at R=3 (by +1.31 and +0.79 points on 0-shot and 1-shot respectively) and at R=5 (by +0.15 and +0.50). The headline small-scale cell is DeepLoop R=5 1-shot at 49.66 %. Against the same-R baseline (base R=5 1-shot, 49.16 %), DeepLoop wins on four of the eight tasks (ARC-C, ARC-E, HellaSwag, WinoGrande), is within 0.11 points on three tasks (PIQA, SciQ, SIQA), and loses clearly only on OpenBookQA. 21 Figure 4 Eight-task 1-shot Avg accuracy against loop count for the GPT-2 small backbone, base versus DeepLoop. Numbers match the 1-shot Avg column of Table 4. Figure 5 Per-task 1-shot accuracy against loop count for base versus DeepLoop on all eight tasks at the GPT-2 small scale. Numbers match Table 4. 22 Empirical p-sweep at fixed loop count Proposition 3.2 predicts sharp p=1/2 training-stability threshold under the worst-case aligned regime. We test this prediction directly with single-axis sweep over the exponent at fixed R=3 on the GPT-2 small backbone. All other ingredients, tied embeddings, input-embedding RMSNorm, FineWeb-Edu 50BT data pipeline, batch size, learning rate, and seed protocol, are identical to the runs in Table 1; only the residual scaling α = (2N )p and initialization gain β = (8N )p change. We run grid of seven values, {0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45, 0.50, 0.55, 0.60}, with up to five seeds per value. Each cell is 90-minute run on 4H200 141 GB GPUs, which under our throughput reaches roughly 2,700 optimizer steps; we report validation loss at the common comparison checkpoint of step 2000 where available, and use the trailing-50-iter mean train loss as surrogate for runs that do not reach post-warmup validation step. Figure 6 Validation-loss snapshot at step 2000 on FineWeb-Edu 50BT for the GPT-2 small backbone, R=3, sweeping the residual-scaling exponent {0.30, . . . , 0.60}. Each marker is one seed. Red crosses are seeds that never escape the unigram-frequency floor ( 7.67 nats); blue circles are seeds that train; the blue line connects the per-p trained-seed mean. Below p=0.45 all seeds diverge; at p=0.45 the boundary is crossed (some seeds train, some do not); at 0.50 training is reliable. The empirical phase boundary lines up with the Proposition 3.2 prediction of p=1/2. Conditional on convergence, larger gives slightly higher loss (less aggressive learning), so p=1/2 is also the smallest that is reliably stable, supporting it as the default DeepLoop choice. The picture in Figure 6 is consistent with the analysis. First, no seed at {0.30, 0.35, 0.40} ever escapes the unigram floor: these clearly sub-threshold runs reproduce the divergence that Proposition 3.2 predicts under the aligned worst case. Second, among seeds that do train, the mean validation loss at the same step rises monotonically as increases across the boundary (3.70 at p=0.45, 3.73 at p=0.50, 3.76 at p=0.55, 3.80 at p=0.60), confirming that more aggressive scaling (smaller p) delivers stronger learning signal whenever it does not destabilize training. Putting these two observations together: p=1/2 is the smallest exponent that is reliably stable across seeds in this regime. We adopt it as the DeepLoop default for that reason. Sub-threshold choices [0.45, 0.50) produce lower trained-seed mean loss when they do train, but with 1/3 of seeds failing to leave the unigram floor before step 2000, they are not safe defaults. 23 The p-sweep is small in scope: it is at one scale (GPT-2 small), one loop depth (R=3), and one optimizer-step budget. Whether the same boundary at p=1/2 applies at larger K, at different normalization placements, or at substantially longer training, is left to future work and discussed in the Limitations paragraph (6).

sections.title: D Compute resources
sections.content: All experiments were run on NVIDIA H200 141 GB GPUs in SLURM cluster. GPT-2 small cells use 4H200, and GPT-2 medium cells use 8H200. Per-cell wall-clock time is read from the corresponding WandB run records; aggregate GPU-hours below are rounded to the nearest hundred. Per-cell cost. Wall-clock time scales roughly linearly with the loop count R: R=1 R=3 R=5 R=7 Small wall-clock (h) Small GPU-hours / cell Medium wall-clock (h) Medium GPU-hours / cell 9 12 95 22 90 32 260 35 145 52 420 50 77 620 Aggregate cost. The reported FineWeb-Edu language-modeling matrix (Tables 1 and 2: 16 cells {base, DeepLoop}{small, medium}R {1, 3, 5, 7}) totals approximately 3,700 H200 GPU-hours. Exploratory experiments not included in the main paper, range of optimization-related variants and additional benchmark sweeps we evaluated during the development of the method and ultimately did not report, add approximately 7,000 GPU-hours. The total project compute is therefore approximately 10,700 H200 GPU-hours.
